:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

A prisoner serving a “time” sentence may not be paroled until they have served at least 1/4 of that sentence. In Montana, a sentencing judge who imposes a state prison term of more than one year may also impose the restriction that the offender is ineligible for parole and participation in the supervised release program while serving the term. Separate sentences must generally run consecutively. If an offender receives a consecutive sentence after incarceration but before a parole ruling is made on the initial sentence, parole eligibility is determined by aggregating the original sentence and the consecutive sentence. However, if the parole ruling on the initial sentence has already been made, the offender will not be eligible for parole on the consecutive sentence until after the offender discharges the original sentence unless a hearing panel orders otherwise. Due to the fact that an offender may still be eligible for parole on the first sentence, the hearing panel may allow the second term to commence for the purposes of calculating parole eligibility. Note, however, that this does not shorten the length of the consecutive sentence.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 680 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 29% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 73% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 94% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 29% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
